title: Welcome
text:
Welcome to this comprehensive teaching resource to help you develop skills in data handling, programming, and statistical analysis using Python.
All materials are structured to be self-guided and modular, supporting both lectures and independent learning. Each section can be explored individually or followed in sequence.





Understand your data environment, tools, platforms, and version control workflows.



Learn how to write Python code, structure your work, and document it effectively.



Import, clean, transform, and manage datasets from simple to complex formats.



Run statistical tests, build models, and explore results through rich visualisations.



Explore Bayesian methods, databases, automation, and dashboards for power users.



Use templates and mini-projects to practice and demonstrate your knowledge.






Source: GitHub Repository
Rendered Site: ggkuhnle.github.io/data-analysis-toolkit-FNS
Run code using Google Colab, Jupyter, or locally on your machine.


📌 Tip: You can dip into any section independently or follow the structure as a progressive course. All notebooks are open-source and available for reuse with attribution.

title: ✨ Just Try It: The Vibe of Programming
text:
Welcome to coding! This notebook is not about getting things perfect — it’s about trying things, seeing what happens, and getting comfortable with Python.
If you’re feeling unsure or scared of “breaking something” — good news: Python is very hard to break. You can always restart and try again.
Let’s experiment 👇
Code
# 🧠 Can you add a string and a number?
"hippo" + 3
Code
# 🐾 What happens if I forget a bracket?
print("Hello Hippo!"
Code
# 🦛 Can I print a hippo emoji?
print("Here's a hippo: 🦛")
Code
# 🔁 What happens if I divide by zero?
1 / 0
Code
# 🔀 Can I change a number into a string?
str(123) + " hippos"

title: ✨ Just Try It: The Vibe of Programming
text:
🎵 Vibe Tips

You don’t need to understand everything at once.
If something breaks, it’s a learning opportunity.
Programming is just thinking + curiosity + patience.
If it works and helps you understand — that’s the vibe ✨

Ready to try more? Head to the Introduction to Python Syntax notebook next!

title: 🔬 T-Tests and ANOVA
text:
Code
from scipy import stats
stats.ttest_ind(df['Height'][:50], df['Height'][50:])

title: 🔁 Git and Version Control
text:
Version control helps track changes to your code and collaborate safely.
Basic Git workflow:
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git push
Use GitHub to store your project online and collaborate with others.

title: 💻 Using Google Colab and Jupyter
text:
Colab is a cloud-based version of Jupyter, ideal for students:

Pros: No setup, cloud-based, easy sharing
Cons: Needs internet, no persistent storage

Local Jupyter is great for offline use and full control:
pip install notebook
jupyter notebook

title: 📓 Notebooks vs Scripts vs Markdown vs Quarto
text:
Format
Best for
Tools




Notebook
Teaching, exploration
Jupyter, Colab


Script
Reproducible pipelines
.py, R scripts


Markdown
Documentation
GitHub, websites


Quarto
Publications & sites
.qmd, quarto render

title: 📦 Installing and Managing Packages
text:
Python uses pip or conda to install libraries:
pip install pandas matplotlib seaborn
You can also use:
!pip install pandas
inside Colab.

Python is widely used in data science because it’s general-purpose, scriptable, and integrates well with tools and APIs.

title: 🌐 What Is a Data Science Environment?
text:
A data science environment refers to the tools, libraries, platforms, and workflows used to analyse data. This includes:

Programming languages (Python, R)
Interactive notebooks (Jupyter, Colab)
Libraries for analysis and plotting
Version control (e.g. Git)
Platforms for collaboration (GitHub, Overleaf, etc.)

title: 📊 Summary Statistics and Distributions
text:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
sns.histplot(df['Height'], kde=True)
plt.show()
